detail: WebhookAdapter properly validates HMAC signatures for all configured routes using timing-safe comparison (hmac.compare_digest). Supports GitHub (X-Hub-Signature-256), GitLab (X-Gitlab-Token), Svix (v1,whsec_), and generic X-Webhook-Signature. Secrets are validated at startup (fail-closed if missing). INSECURE_NO_AUTH flag exists for local dev only and is blocked from starting on non-loopback binds. Dynamic routes are re-validated on reload.

WebhookAdapter implements per-route rate limiting with a fixed-window algorithm (default 30/minute, configurable). Idempotency cache prevents duplicate agent runs on webhook retries using delivery ID tracking with TTL.

issue: Body size limit enforced before reading — PASS
detail: WebhookAdapter checks Content-Length header against max_body_bytes (default 1MB) BEFORE reading the body. Prevents large-payload DoS attacks.

SmsAdapter validates X-Twilio-Signature header using HMAC-SHA1 via _validate_twilio_signature and _check_signature. Uses hmac.compare_digest for timing-safe comparison. Tries both URL with/without default port since Twilio may sign either way. SMS_INSECURE_NO_SIGNATURE is dev-only and requires SMS_WEBHOOK_URL to be set (i.e., only for local testing scenarios).

issue: Webhook secret required at startup — PASS (with documentation)
detail: TelegramAdapter in webhook mode REQUIRES TELEGRAM_WEBHOOK_SECRET env var. If not set, raises RuntimeError at startup with clear security advisory pointing to GHSA-3vpc-7q5r-276h. This prevents the python-telegram-bot library from running in fail-open auth-bypass mode. Polling mode clears stale webhooks before starting.

FeishuAdapter._is_webhook_signature_valid uses timing-safe hmac.compare_digest for signature verification. Additionally has verification token check as second defense layer. Headers checked: x-lark-request-timestamp, x-lark-request-nonce, x-lark-signature.
WeComCrypto.verify_url properly validates msg_signature against expected SHA1 signature. decrypt() raises SignatureError on mismatch.

issue: Bridge subprocess on localhost:3000 — LOW RISK
detail: WhatsApp adapter spawns a Node.js bridge subprocess communicating over HTTP on bridge_port (default 3000) with no authentication mechanism on the bridge HTTP interface. The bridge is only accessible on localhost. However, any local process that can reach localhost:3000 can inject messages into the WhatsApp adapter. This is mitigated by default deployment assumptions (localhost access is trusted), but represents a local privilege escalation vector if another user/process on the same machine can reach the bridge port.

issue: bridge_script path from config — LOW RISK
detail: The bridge_script path is user-configurable via config.extra (default: scripts/whatsapp-bridge/bridge.js). A misconfigured or malicious config could point to an arbitrary script. However, this requires existing access to modify config.yaml or environment variables.

issue: Socket Mode — authentication handled by Slack — PASS
detail: Slack adapter uses Socket Mode (App-level token xapp-... via SLACK_APP_TOKEN). Authentication is handled entirely by the Slack SDK over a persistent WebSocket connection. No manual signature verification needed — Slack delivers only authenticated events to the registered handler. Bot tokens stored in slack_tokens.json with platform lock (600 mode). Tokens are redacted in logs (only last 4 chars shown). No webhook-based inbound auth to implement.

issue: discord_tool.py is an agent tool, not a gateway adapter — PASS
detail: tools/discord_tool.py is a server introspection tool exposed to the agent, not a gateway platform adapter receiving inbound webhooks. It makes outbound REST API calls using the DISCORD_BOT_TOKEN from environment variables. No incoming webhook signature validation applies. Token is retrieved via os.getenv and used in Bot {token} Authorization header. Token is not logged or exposed in error messages.

issue: Outbound HTTP via urllib.request.urlopen — NO SSRF PROTECTION
detail: Several platform adapters (wecom.py, feishu.py) use urllib.request.urlopen for outbound API calls without explicit URL allowlisting. These are outbound calls to known API endpoints (WeCom API, Feishu onboard API) rather than user-controlled callback URLs, but there is no enforced URL validation. In practice, SSRF risk is mitigated by the fact that these call fixed已知 API domains rather than arbitrary user-provided URLs.

issue: Session path stored in hermes directory — PASS
detail: WhatsApp session data (includes bridge state) is stored under get_hermes_dir('platforms/whatsapp/session', 'whatsapp/session') which is profile-aware. bridge.pid file used for cleanup. _kill_stale_bridge_by_pidfile properly validates PID before sending signal. No credential leakage in process management.
